content:
3 min read
1 hour ago

--

Your JavaScript isn’t broken. It’s just running too early.
If you’ve ever tried to move, add, or remove a widget element in Mendix using JavaScript — and hit a silent failure like “element not found” — you’re not alone.
This isn’t a logic problem. It’s a timing problem. 🕑
Mendix renders its UI asynchronously. By the time your script runs, the DOM element you’re targeting may not exist yet.
So your querySelector returns null, your manipulation never runs, and you get no error to debug. ⁉️
The fix is MutationObserver — a native browser API that watches the DOM and fires your code the moment your target element actually appears. No setTimeout hacks. No polling loops. No guessing.
Here’s how to set it up in three steps.
Step 1 — Name the Widget You Want to Target 🎯
In Mendix Studio Pro, every widget has a Name property in the Common section of its properties panel.
Give your source widget a clear, identifiable name. In this example, I’ve named it MovableHeader.
properties panel with Name set to “MovableHeader”This name becomes a CSS class on the rendered DOM element, prefixed with mx-name-. So MovableHeader becomes .mx-name-MovableHeader in the browser.
Step 2 — Add an HTML Snippet Widget to Your Page
Place an HTML Snippet widget on the same page where you want to do the DOM manipulation.
In its properties: Set Content Type to JavaScript. This is where you’ll write your custom JS code.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

MutationObserver code in the HtmlSnippet WidgetStep 3 — Use MutationObserver to Run Your DOM Logic
The code below is one example — it moves all child elements from a source widget into a target widget. Your actual DOM manipulation will be different depending on your use case. What stays the same is the MutationObserver pattern wrapping it.
Here’s the example to understand the structure👨‍💻:
// sets up a watcher(MutationObserver).
// The callback fires every time child elements change anywhere in the DOM.
const observer = new MutationObserver(() => {// Your code for DOM manipulation
const source = document.querySelector(".mx-name-MovableHeader");
const target = document.querySelector(".mx-name-TargetHeader .tr");
if (source && target) {
while (source.firstElementChild) {
target.appendChild(source.firstElementChild);
}
observer.disconnect(); // Stop watching once the job is done
}
});
// starts watching the entire document for DOM changes like any child element additions or removals.
observer.observe(document.body, { childList: true, subtree: true });
Why This Works
Mendix’s rendering pipeline doesn’t guarantee that a widget is in the DOM when the page first loads. MutationObserver sidesteps this entirely — instead of assuming the element exists, it waits and reacts the moment it does.
This is more reliable than setTimeout because: setTimeout is a guess.
Slow devices or heavy pages will break it.
MutationObserver is event-driven. It fires precisely when the condition is met, regardless of how long rendering takes.
Things to Watch Out For 👀

• Always call observer.disconnect() once your manipulation is complete. A perpetually running observer can cause performance issues.

• CSS class names are case-sensitive .mx-name-MovableHeader and .mx-name-movableheader are not the same.
For more information on MutationObserver please refer the below documentation 🙂
